title: The Field Knows What It Wants
statement:
Empty editor. Build the form shown in the target on the right.

Three fields. On screen all three are the same white rectangle — there's no way to tell them apart by looking.

But one is waiting for a name, another for an email, the third for a phone number. And all three can say so themselves.
constraints: Three fields: Your name, Email, Phone
Every field has its own label, tied to it
Every field is of the right kind and has a name
Without an email the form doesn't go

checks.t7: There is a free text field
checks.t8: The fields are tied to their labels
checks.t15: There is a sentence below the heading

why.empty: The editor is empty. There is no starter code here — you build the form yourself.
why.not-closed:
Some tag is opened and never closed.

Input fields have no closing tag. The form, the labels and the button do.
why.no-heading: There is no heading, or it is empty.
why.no-form: The fields are there, but not inside a form. The form is what gathers them and sends them together.
why.few-fields: There aren't three fields in the form. Name, email and phone make three.
why.no-email-type:
There is no email field.

Left as free text, it accepts anything — including "dunno". And you get rubbish.

Once the field says what it wants, the browser checks it for you, and a phone opens a keyboard with the @ sign in plain view.
why.no-tel-type:
There is no phone field.

On a phone the visitor gets a letter keyboard and has to hunt for the digits. Three letters save them that.
why.no-text-type:
There is no free text field.

A name is a name — it has no fixed shape and shouldn't be checked by anyone.
why.no-labels:
There is a field with no label tied to it.

You've written the word next to the field — a sighted person sees it, and that misleads you into thinking all is well.

But a label isn't text next to a field. It's a LINK. A blind visitor focuses the field and the screen reader stays silent: "edit field, blank".
why.few-labels: There aren't three labels. Every field wants its own.
why.empty-label: There is an empty label. The word has to be inside it.
why.no-name:
There is a field with no name, or with an empty one.

Data travels as name-value pairs. A field with no name arrives nowhere — the server drops it silently.

Note: this isn't the same as the label. The label is for the person, the name is for the server.
why.no-required:
The email isn't required.

Without that, someone sends an empty form and you get nothing — and then you have no way to reply.

One attribute with no value stops the submission, and the browser points at the field itself.
why.no-button: There is no button. The form can be filled in, but not sent.
why.no-text: There is no sentence below the heading, or it is empty.

no-email-type: An input field has an attribute that says what's expected inside it.
Look at each field's name to tell which is which. The kind follows the meaning, not the order.
Lesson 57: email wants a five-letter word.
no-tel-type: The phone has its own kind too.
Three letters. Not telephone and not phone.
no-labels: A label and a field are tied through a shared name. One points, the other is called that.
There are two correct ways: either the label points at the field's name, or it wraps the field entirely.
Three fields, three links. One broken is enough.
no-required: There's an attribute with no value — it simply exists or it doesn't.
You don't write it with a value of "true". Its presence is the value.
It goes on the email field — without that you can't reply to the person.

solution.why:
The first instinct is to drop in three fields and write a word above each. On screen you get exactly what you wanted — a form.

But the three fields are identical only in appearance. One waits for a name, another for an email, the third for a phone — and each of them can say so itself.

Once it does, you get three things at once without writing a line: the browser validates for you, the phone opens the right keyboard, and the visitor sees their mistake before sending.

And the second invisible thing: the word above a field isn't a label. It's text standing nearby. A label is a link — and without it the screen reader says only "edit field".
steps: Start with the heading and the sentence below it.
Open the form — it gathers the fields and sends them together.
For each field, write the label before it and tie the two through a shared name.
Now go through the fields and ask about each: what exactly is it waiting for?
Give each one the right kind. Follow the meaning, not the order.
Give each one a name too — that's for the server, not for the person.
The email is required. The attribute is one word, with no value.
Finally the button. Without it the form fills in but never leaves.
solution.code:
Photo walk

Sign up and we will send you the meeting point.
solution.note:
And a warning: the validation you just got for free is a CONVENIENCE, not protection.

Anyone can remove it with two clicks in their own browser. It protects the person from their own mistake — it doesn't protect you from a bad one. The server checks again, always.
